data.title: Unum: Another Key Milestone Supports Ongoing Multiple Expansion
data.description:
Shares of Unum Group (NYSE:UNM) have been an excellent performer over the past year, adding about 60% as the company's growth businesses are performing well while fears around its legacy, toxic insurance policies have lessened. On Tuesday, July 1st, Unum closed a significant transaction that further accelerates its transformation. I last covered Unum in March, reiterating shares as a "buy." Since then, they have added 3%, but the market is up 10%. With it closing its reinsurance deal, now is a good time to revisit UNM.

Fortitude Re Deal Reduces Closed Block Risk

On Tuesday, Unum closed its agreement with Fortitude Re. Under this reinsurance deal, Unum is ceding $3.4 billion of long-term care reserves to the reinsurer, which represents about 19% of the company's total in-force long-term care (LTC) block as well as 20% of its individual disability block. This deal will unlock about $100 million of capital with a $200 million capital hit on LTC offset by a $300 million disability benefit. Unum can take this capital and either redeploy it into its core business, where it is generating 20+% returns on equity, or return that capital to shareholders.

Frankly, I believe more of the value for UNM shareholders in this transaction is shedding the 19% of risk, more so than the $100 million capital release. I would still view this deal positively even if it were capital neutral. LTC insurance has been a disaster for essentially every player involved in the market, as policies were severely underpriced in the 1990s-2000s. This has led companies like UNM to segregate LTC into a "closed block" and seek to grow the business elsewhere.

Given how consistently these policies have underperformed expectations, there has been an ongoing concern that UNM could need to inject more capital to support future claims. By reinsuring 19% of the business and doing so in an accretive way, the likelihood that UNM will need to provide additional support is much lower. With this transaction, investors can focus increasingly on the rest of the business and less on LTC.

I Value UNM Excluding the Closed Block's Equity

Still, this will be an ongoing and gradual process. Of its $11.2 billion in book equity, $3.6 billion sits in the closed block. The closed block has the majority of the assets, at $33 billion out of $62 billion. Given this transaction closed on July 1st, we should see a step-down in Closed Block exposure in Q3 results. My underlying assumption continues to be that the equity in the Closed Block has no value for UNM shareholders.

Essentially, I expect that equity to be needed to support ongoing claims. To the extent that UNM has excess equity it can eventually withdraw, it is likely years away given how long-term LTC contracts are. Importantly, I do not see UNM needing to inject more capital to support these policies, and this deal makes that an even more remote risk. Still, I would not assign any value to the unit.

This is important because such a substantial portion of Unum's consolidated book value includes the Closed Block. Unum has a $76 book value excluding accumulated other comprehensive income. However, excluding the closed block and unrealized losses, book value stands at $49. I view $49 as the more relevant book value metrics, since it is the rest of the business that ultimately is supporting UNM's dividend and buybacks.

Q1 Was Mixed, but Issues Should Be Temporary

While this is a significant movement that helps Unum move beyond its LTC challenges, I would note that recent performance in the remaining "core" insurance business has been a bit more mixed. In the company's first quarter reported on April 29th, Unum earned $2.04, which missed expectations by $0.15 as premiums grew by just under 4% to $2.7 billion. Colonial Life was a bright spot as it earned $116 million with sales rising by 2%.

The company faced two primary challenges. First, claims were higher than last year. Policy benefits rose by $116 million, whereas premiums grew by just $92 million. As a result, Unum US earnings declined by 15% to $329 million. Premiums grew by 4%, but disability benefits rose to 61.8% from 57.5% last year. Last year's underwriting was extremely strong, which created some of the unfavorability. Management targets a low-60% benefit ratio, so this outcome was still consistent with targets.

Beyond the normalization in underwriting, net investment income was stable at $513 million. The earned book yield fell to 4.25% from 4.44%, which was a surprisingly large decline, given the reinvestment environment remains favorable. UNM's portfolio is fairly conservative. It includes $35.8 billion of fixed income $1.7 billion of alternatives. As you can see, the fixed income portfolio is fairly conservative, with 96% rated investment grade.

However, its alternatives portfolio delivered poor results. Alts earned 5.1%, and as noted on the earnings call, Q1 results can be seasonally weaker given delays in getting 12/31 valuations. Notably, even in late April when the stock market was substantially lower, management reiterated its 8-10% return expectation. With asset valuations substantially higher today, this guidance is even more credible, and I expect to see alternative returns improve meaningfully in Q2 and Q3.

UNM is Attractive Given Strong Capital Returns and Attractive Multiple

Importantly, Unum is very well capitalized. Risk-based capital ("RBC") is now at 460%, well above the 400-425% I like to see. Unum also has $2.2 billion of holding company liquidity. As such, UNM can aggressively return capital to investors. On May 22nd, Unum announced it will raise its dividend by nearly 10% to $0.46, starting in the third quarter. That gives shares a 2.3% go-forward dividend yield. It also did $200 million of Q1 buybacks, and as a result, its share count is down over 7% from last year. We should see at least $200 million of buybacks in Q2.

Back in March, I was targeting $1.3 billion of capital returns and $9 of EPS. Depending on the speed of share repurchases, I expect capital returns to come in at $1.2-$1.3 billion. I also expect much of the Q1 miss to prove transitory given the likely rebound in alternatives income, which should result in ~$9.00 of EPS. In theory, with a 20% core ROE, UNM could trade 2x book value and still provide a 10% return for investors, suggesting upside into the upper $90's, considering my $49 adjusted book value.

In practice, I believe a lower price target is appropriate. While the overhang from LTC is diminishing thanks in part to this week's announcement, there clearly is still some overhang and risk associated with these policies. I also expect it reinsured some of the better policies, with more toxic exposure still being retained. While most insurers are trading in the 11-13x zone, I continue to view about 10x earnings as more reasonable.

That still points to an upside of about 11% to $90, and with UNM repurchasing 6-7% of its shares each year, there continues to be technical support for shares. With a single-digit multiple, I view these buybacks as accretive, and they will support meaningful EPS growth. With Closed Block pressure declining, UNM should enjoy ongoing, but gradual multiple expansion as the overhang diminishes, and I view shares as attractive at 9x earnings.
